input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is a 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle configured as a teleoperated test platform for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). It consists of an advanced sensor suite (including front‑mounted cameras and lidar/radar for object detection), a resource‑constrained on‑board controller unit typical of automotive ECUs, and a dual‑layer chassis architecture (lower and upper). Operating within controlled indoor/outdoor test tracks, the vehicle’s scalable size, rich sensor integration, and safety‑critical design make it well suited for Digital Twin monitoring, computation offloading, and remote management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers three primary services: 1) Data Monitoring – a web‑based UI streams real‑time vehicle state data, projected trajectory, and nearby object tracks; users can view, save, and generate telemetry reports. 2) Computation Offloading – the DT hosts the ACC algorithm on cloud/edge compute, receives sensor data from the vehicle, calculates the lead‑vehicle distance, derives a desired speed, and pushes this command back to the on‑board ECU, alleviating on‑board computational load. 3) Remote Management – operators can remotely enable/disable ACC, set a target speed, or issue an emergency‑braking command through the UI; the service ensures low end‑to‑end latency, real‑time monitoring, and remote diagnostics.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The twin operates on a 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy, mandated by safety‑critical vehicular standards, to support computation off‑loading among devices. A temporal granularity of 100 ms is the baseline for all digital‑physical interactions, ensuring that sensor data, control commands, and model updates are exchanged within this bound. Latency requirements are implicit in maintaining this 100 ms window, with any communication or processing delays exceeding this bound considered unacceptable for real‑time vehicle control.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system defines a hierarchical structure of three principal entities: a User Entity, a Digital Twin Entity, and a Physical Twin Entity. Each entity is instantiated on separate hardware components (e.g., edge compute nodes for the User Entity, cloud or vehicle‑bound compute nodes for the Digital Twin Entity, and on‑board vehicle hardware for the Physical Twin Entity). No explicit multiple twin instances are described, and the architecture is effectively decentralized, with each entity performing its own responsibilities and communicating over ROS2 topics. Coordination between entities is achieved through ROS2 message passing and interfaces defined by the ISO 23247 Functional Entities (FEs).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows ISO 23247 Reference Architecture, mapping SW functional entities (FEs) onto individual ROS 2 packages that encapsulate ROS 2 nodes implementing the entity behaviours. The User Entity contains the User Interface FE split into two sub‑components (3D Visualization and Control Panel). The Digital Twin Entity hosts data‑management and control FEs, while the Physical Twin Entity incorporates data pre‑processing, actuation, and device‑communication FEs such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking. The orchestration is decentralized across hardware nodes, with resource management handled by ROS 2’s node lifecycle and package deployment on each hardware platform. System‑wide coordination is achieved via ROS 2 topic buses, service calls, and action servers, ensuring synchronous updates between physical devices and virtual models.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw point‑cloud data generated by the vehicle’s LiDAR sensor is transmitted over a Wi‑Fi link that uses UDP as the transport protocol. Within the digital twin, the raw data is processed by the Point‑Cloud Clustering & Tracking component, which clusters individual LiDAR points into coherent object tracks and computes a projected driving path. All sensor streams (LiDAR, vehicle state, steering, and motor data) are then funneled through the Data Translation component, which serializes the data, aligns message types according to ISO 23247, performs unit conversions, and prepares the payloads for downstream services. The transformed data is distributed across a ROS 2 network (DDS‑based) to subscribing nodes such as the real‑time 3‑D Visualization module. The Data Monitoring service consumes the processed data to display vehicle state, object tracks, and projected paths in real time. The document does not specify explicit transmission frequencies; the system operates in continuous streaming mode sufficient for real‑time monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin System implements a dual‑layer communication architecture. Intra‑system component interactions are handled by a ROS 2 network that uses the Data Distribution Service (DDS) middleware, providing publish‑subscribe messaging over UDP with typical multicast or unicast configurations. Inter‑entity communication between the physical twin (sensors/actuators) and the digital twin occurs over a conventional Wi‑Fi network using UDP as the transport protocol. All transmitted messages are routed through the Data Translation component, which serializes payloads, normalizes message types, and executes unit conversions to guarantee consistency across the system. The documentation does not provide explicit bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security specifications for this network infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system adheres to ISO 23247:2021 Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing, implementing the reference architecture defined in Part 2. This architecture enumerates four primary entities: Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity, each composed of Functional Entities (FEs) that handle specific tasks such as device control, data transformation, and user interface provision. Industrial communication between the twin and the physical manufacturing elements is conducted via OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA), ensuring secure, bidirectional data exchange compatible with IEC 61131‑3 PLC stacks. The open‑source reference implementation hosted on GitHub demonstrates these components in an automotive manufacturing context, and a public YouTube demonstration confirms runtime behaviour. By following ISO 23247, the twin satisfies interoperability, security, and data‑modelling requirements for digital twins in manufacturing environments.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: A comprehensive security and safety framework is implemented in the Digital Twin system, based on the requirements of ISO 15622, 3GPP TS 22.185, ISO 26262‑1 and the model‑driven architecture proposed by Dalibor et al. For cyber‑security, the system adopts a PKI‑based authentication mechanism using X.509 certificates issued to every V2X node, and establishes secure communication channels with DTLS 1.2 over UDP. All exchange of control and status data is encrypted with AES‑128‑GCM and integrity‑protected by SHA‑256 based HMAC. Access control is enforced through role‑based permissions defined in the system‑model, and data flows are logged for auditability. For functional safety, ISO 26262‑1 mandates a full Safety Lifecycle, including Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment, to assign ASIL levels; safety‑critical functions such as adaptive cruise control deactivate in an ASIL D compliant fail‑safe mode when fault detection signals from multiple sensors (radar, lidar, camera) indicate a conflict. Redundancy in the control loop and a watchdog timer ensure timely recovery, and the Digital Twin maintains a Safety Integrity Level SIL 3 for the brake‑intervention logic. The architecture of the twin, described in the model‑driven cockpit study, explicitly models safety constraints in SysML, and the twin’s self‑diagnostic components fulfill the verification and validation actions required by ISO 26262‑1. Together these measures guarantee that the Digital Twin meets both cybersecurity and functional‑safety requirements while providing a resilient and trustworthy operation.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
